Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to dedicate around 30 hours a week, but it never felt organized in a traditional sense. I'd usually study late at night when my roommates were asleep because that's when I could focus without distractions. I relied heavily on reading and writing things out by hand since that's how information sticks for me. The routine existed, but it was more about finding pockets of time than following a strict schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a bit each day, but then other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Working with limited resources means I sometimes had to prioritize other responsibilities over sticking to a perfect study schedule. Deadlines were usually what dictated when I'd really buckle down and focus intensely.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew an assignment was coming up or when I needed to check if anything new was posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a deadline reminder, and that would push me to check in. Honestly, if there wasn't something specific I needed, I wouldn't just browse around the LMS for fun. It felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something I engaged with regularly.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my daily routine. I'd go through phases where I logged in more frequently, especially early in the semester when I was trying to stay on top of things. But as the course went on and I started feeling more bored with the content, it became something I only used when absolutely necessary. The peer support group helped remind me to check in sometimes, but it never felt automatic.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and lecture notes and work through them on my own time. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the instructor. Videos were okay, but I found myself preferring to read transcripts when they were available because I could go at my own pace.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the video content after a while because watching long lectures felt tedious and I'd zone out. The interactive quizzes that weren't graded also got skipped because they felt like busywork when I had actual assignments to complete. Some of the supplementary resources seemed interesting but with everything else I had going on, they just fell to the bottom of my priority list. If it wasn't directly tied to my grade, it was hard to justify spending time on it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or actually understanding something I was struggling with. When I could read through material and take notes that I knew would help on an exam, that felt productive. The peer support sessions were valuable because talking through problems with other students made concepts click in ways that just reading alone didn't. Anything that felt like it was just checking a box without real learning felt like a waste.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely different because there was no real consequence for skipping them. With required work, even when I was bored or stressed, I knew I had to push through somehow. Optional stuff was the first thing to go when time got tight or when my motivation dipped. I think if I had more energy and fewer competing demands, I might have engaged with optional content more, but this semester that just wasn't realistic for me.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download readings and notes, then print them out at the library when I could afford it or just read them on my phone without being connected to the LMS. I filled notebooks with handwritten summaries and practice problems. I also used free resources I found online, like YouTube explanations and open textbooks, because sometimes they explained things better than our course materials.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours working through problems in my notebook or re-reading printed materials, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. When I was preparing for exams, I'd review my handwritten notes repeatedly rather than logging back into the LMS. My peer study sessions, even the informal ones where we'd text about concepts, wouldn't register as any kind of activity. The LMS only saw a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then work with those materials for days without logging back in. It saved time and also helped because my internet connection isn't always reliable. Once I had the PDFs and notes saved locally, there was no reason to keep going back to the LMS unless something new was posted. My actual engagement with the content happened entirely offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was stressed about money, working extra hours when I could find work, and the course content was getting more complex. I started feeling genuinely bored with the material, which made it even harder to force myself to engage. My attendance dropped because I couldn't always make it to class, and catching up online felt overwhelming. The peer support helped me not give up entirely, but I was definitely struggling.
Q13: All of those factors hit me at different points. Fatigue was constant because I wasn't sleeping well due to stress. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections rather than tackle them head-on. When work or family stuff demanded my attention, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I tried to stay resilient and keep pushing through, but there were definitely weeks where I barely touched the course at all because life got in the way.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open up readings or start watching a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what anyone was saying. It's like my body was going through the motions of studying, but mentally I was checked out. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though technically I was 'engaging' with the materials.
Q15: I definitely avoided the assignments that felt most confusing or overwhelming. Even knowing they were important, the anxiety of not understanding would make me put them off until the last possible moment. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was in a better headspace, but tomorrow would come and I'd still feel the same. It wasn't laziness exactly—it was more like a mental block that I couldn't push through until the deadline forced me to.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that consistently got me to log in and submit work. I'd watch the due dates approach and feel that pressure building, which would finally override the boredom and stress. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered right before things were due. It's not the healthiest pattern, but when motivation is low and everything else is competing for attention, deadlines become the main driver of action.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on assignments, it did boost my motivation temporarily. Knowing that my written work was scoring well made me feel like maybe I could pull through this course. But feedback was sometimes slow to come, and by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. The peer support feedback was more immediate and honestly more helpful for keeping me engaged day-to-day.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my situation. Having set times for things when my schedule was unpredictable made attendance really difficult. The way content was released weekly meant if I fell behind, it was hard to catch up without feeling overwhelmed. I appreciated that materials stayed available, but the pacing assumed a consistency that I just couldn't maintain with everything else going on in my life.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content made engagement harder for me since I'm more of a read-write learner. When there were good written summaries or transcripts, that made things much easier. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, like I was posting just to meet a quota rather than because I had something meaningful to say. Having peer support available was one design element that actually helped because it gave me human connection and accountability.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reading everything, watching videos, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, something breaks down. Maybe they don't understand the material well enough, or anxiety prevents them from completing things, or life circumstances interrupt their ability to finish. High activity doesn't always translate to high output, especially when someone is fighting to stay afloat.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat, though my exam score didn't reflect it well. My assignment scores were quite high when I actually submitted work, which came from deep offline studying rather than LMS activity. Someone could absolutely learn effectively through downloaded materials, textbooks, or study groups without the LMS showing much. The system only captures one narrow slice of how learning actually happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and whether you submit things on time. That's useful for identifying students who might be completely disengaged. But logs miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss offline work entirely, and they can't capture someone's mental state or the external factors affecting their participation. It's like looking at footprints without seeing the journey.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you genuinely want to discuss ideas with peers. For me, engagement also means persistence—showing up mentally even when you're bored or stressed or would rather be doing anything else. It's a combination of intellectual investment and consistent effort, even when it's hard.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the late nights with my notebook, the stress about whether I could afford to stay in school, or the genuine effort I put into assignments even when my motivation was gone. It wouldn't show that the peer support connections meant more to me than any metric could measure, or that failing this course doesn't mean I stopped trying. The data shows outcomes, not the struggle behind them.
